title: SEGMENT 6 — From the bench to the bedside: how susceptibility is actually measured

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — From the bench to the bedside: how susceptibility is actually measured
I introduced the MIC a moment ago as the central number in antibiotic use, but I gave it to you as an abstraction.

Let me make it concrete, because understanding how we actually measure whether a drug works against a given patient's bug ties the pharmacology to what happens in a real hospital lab, and it'll make the resistance discussion at the end much more tangible.
When a patient has a serious infection, the lab doesn't just guess which antibiotic to use.

They culture the organism — grow it from a blood sample, a urine sample, a wound swab — and then they test that specific organism against a panel of antibiotics to see which ones inhibit it and at what concentration.
This is antibiotic susceptibility testing, and there are a few classic methods worth knowing.
The oldest and most visual is the disk diffusion method, often called Kirby-Bauer.
You spread the bacteria evenly across the surface of an agar plate so they'd grow into a uniform lawn.
Then you place small paper disks on the surface, each impregnated with a known antibiotic.
As the plate incubates, each antibiotic diffuses outward from its disk into the surrounding agar, creating a concentration gradient — high right next to the disk, fading to nothing further away.
Where the drug concentration is high enough to inhibit growth, no bacteria grow; further out, where the drug has thinned to ineffective levels, the bacteria grow normally.
The result is a clear circle — a zone of inhibition — around each effective disk.
Measure the diameter of that clear zone: a big zone means the drug is potent against this organism, a small zone or no zone means it isn't working.
It's a beautifully simple readout — you can literally see susceptibility with your eyes — though by itself it gives you a category, susceptible or resistant, more than a precise number.
To get an actual MIC number, the classic method is broth dilution.
You set up a row of tubes or wells, each containing the same bacteria but a different concentration of the antibiotic — typically doubling each step: one unit, two, four, eight, sixteen, and so on.
You incubate them, and then you look at which tubes turned cloudy.
Cloudy means the bacteria grew; clear means they didn't.
You scan up the series until you find the lowest concentration that kept the tube clear — that's the lowest concentration that inhibited visible growth, and that, by definition, is the MIC.
Then, if you want the MBC — the bactericidal concentration — you take samples from the clear tubes, where nothing grew, and try to subculture them onto fresh drug-free plates.
If bacteria grow back, they were only inhibited, not killed — merely stunned.
The lowest concentration from which essentially nothing grows back is the MBC, because at that concentration the bugs weren't just frozen, they were dead.
And there it is, made physical: the gap between MIC and MBC is the gap between freezing and killing, which we can literally measure in a row of tubes.

There's a hybrid method many labs love called the gradient strip — a plastic strip laid on an agar lawn that carries a pre-formed gradient of antibiotic, marked with a concentration scale printed right on it.
The zone of inhibition forms an elliptical shape around the strip, and you simply read off the MIC at the point where the edge of the bacterial growth intersects the strip's scale.
It combines the visual simplicity of disk diffusion with an actual numeric MIC.
Now, here's the part that connects all this back to the pharmacology and stops it from being mere lab trivia.
Once you have an MIC, the question is: does it matter?
An MIC is a number from a test tube.
The patient is not a test tube.
So the lab compares the MIC against a published threshold called a breakpoint — and reports the organism as susceptible, intermediate, or resistant.
But where do those breakpoints come from?

They come from exactly the pharmacodynamic concepts we built earlier: they're set with reference to the drug concentrations we can actually achieve in a patient's blood and tissues at standard doses, and to the killing pattern of the drug.
For a time-dependent killer like a beta-lactam, the breakpoint reflects whether we can keep the level above that MIC for enough of the dosing interval.
For a concentration-dependent killer like an aminoglycoside, it reflects whether we can get the peak high enough above the MIC.
So the breakpoint is where the in-the-tube number, the MIC, meets the in-the-patient reality of pharmacokinetics and the mechanism of killing.

That's the whole chain: mechanism dictates the killing pattern, the killing pattern dictates the dosing strategy, the dosing strategy determines achievable concentrations, and those concentrations set the breakpoint that tells the clinician whether the MIC is good enough.
I'm laboring this because it's the moment where the molecular biology we're about to do becomes clinical medicine.
Every time you'll later hear "this organism is resistant," what that means underneath is: its MIC has climbed past the concentration we can safely achieve in the patient.

And why the MIC climbed will always be one of the four resistance mechanisms we'll cover at the end — the bug changed its target, or learned to destroy the drug, or stopped letting it in, or started pumping it out.
The lab report is the visible tip; the mechanism is the iceberg beneath.
Keep that linkage in mind, and now — finally — let's open up the drugs themselves, starting with the cell wall.
